---
pagetitle: "invincible_proj | F1TENTH Time Trial"
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    page-layout: full
---

::: {.hero-section .hero-racing}

::: {.hero-badge}
ECE 484 Final Project · F1TENTH Time Trial
:::

# invincible_proj: Autonomous F1TENTH Time Trial Racing {.title}

::: {.subtitle}
Designing a fast, reliable, and competition-ready autonomous race car on a stock F1TENTH platform using onboard sensing, perception, planning, and control.
:::

::: {.author-list}
[**Zhenyu Zhang**](https://example.com)^1^,
[**Yuanzhe Wang**](https://example.com)^1^,
[**Murphy Huang**](https://example.com)^1^
:::

::: {.affiliation-list}
^1^University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign · ECE 484 Autonomous Vehicles
:::

::: {.button-row}
[[{{< fa video >}} Demo Video]{.btn-text}](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cSQTZoZPJzs){.btn .btn-primary}
[[{{< fa brands github >}} GitHub]{.btn-text}](https://github.com/safeautonomy-illinois-students/project-site-invincible_proj/){.btn .btn-primary}
[[{{< fa flag-checkered >}} System]{.btn-text}](#system){.btn .btn-primary}
[[{{< fa chart-line >}} Results]{.btn-text}](#results){.btn .btn-primary}
:::

::: {.hero-highlight-grid}
::: {.hero-highlight-card}
<div class="highlight-label">Goal</div>
<div class="highlight-value">Finish the track quickly and cleanly</div>
<div class="highlight-note">Optimize for speed, stability, and lap consistency in a one-car time trial setting.</div>
:::

::: {.hero-highlight-card}
<div class="highlight-label">Sensor Stack</div>
<div class="highlight-value">LiDAR · Camera · IMU</div>
<div class="highlight-note">Use any combination of stock onboard sensors without changing hardware.</div>
:::

::: {.hero-highlight-card}
<div class="highlight-label">Core Challenge</div>
<div class="highlight-value">Perception to control under tight latency</div>
<div class="highlight-note">Every module must work robustly enough for real-time autonomous driving on a race track.</div>
:::
:::

:::

::: {.section-container .section-soft}

::: {.hero-summary-panel}
<div class="summary-kicker">Project Snapshot</div>
<div class="summary-text">
This project focuses on autonomous racing for the <strong>F1TENTH Time Trial</strong>. The car must drive around a closed track as quickly as possible using only the sensors and compute already available on the platform. No hardware modifications are allowed, so performance depends almost entirely on software design: how well we perceive the track, estimate vehicle state, choose a drivable path, and command steering and speed in real time.
</div>
<div class="chip-row">
<span class="info-chip">Real vehicle</span>
<span class="info-chip">Software-only improvements</span>
<span class="info-chip">Race against the clock</span>
<span class="info-chip">Safety + performance</span>
</div>
:::

:::

::: {#overview .section-container}

## Overview {.section-title}

::: {.abstract-text}
Our goal is to build an autonomous F1TENTH race car that can complete a time-trial course quickly, smoothly, and repeatably on a stock vehicle. Unlike a simulation-only pipeline, this project must handle sensor noise, imperfect calibration, limited computation, and real-time control constraints. A strong solution is not just fast on average; it must also remain stable near turns, recover from small trajectory errors, and avoid oscillatory steering that wastes time.

We approach the problem as a full autonomy stack. The vehicle uses onboard sensing to understand the nearby environment, converts that information into a track-relative driving objective, and then executes that objective through steering and speed control. Depending on the final system design, this can include LiDAR-based free-space reasoning, camera-based track understanding, IMU-assisted stabilization, or a hybrid fusion strategy. The final website is structured to show not only the racing result, but also the engineering decisions behind the result: sensing, planning, control logic, evaluation strategy, and lessons learned from real-car testing.
:::

:::

::: {.section-band}
::: {#challenge .section-container}

## Why Time Trial Is Interesting {.section-title}

::: {.challenge-grid}
::: {.challenge-card}
### Speed vs. Stability
A faster controller is not always better. Aggressive throttle and steering can reduce lap time in theory, but in practice they often increase drift, overshoot, and recovery time.
:::

::: {.challenge-card}
### Perception Under Noise
LiDAR returns can be sparse near sharp geometry, cameras depend on lighting and viewpoint, and IMU signals drift over time. The algorithm must stay reliable when the data is imperfect.
:::

::: {.challenge-card}
### Real-Time Constraints
The autonomy stack must run onboard with low latency. A great planner that reacts too slowly can perform worse than a simpler method that updates quickly and consistently.
:::

::: {.challenge-card}
### Race-Day Robustness
A competition system needs more than one good run. It should tolerate small disturbances, recover from minor mistakes, and maintain repeatable performance across repeated laps.
:::
:::

:::
:::

::: {#system .section-container}

## System Pipeline {.section-title}

::: {.content-text}
The final implementation can follow several successful F1TENTH strategies, but most strong systems share the same high-level structure shown below.
:::

::: {.pipeline-grid}
::: {.pipeline-step}
<div class="step-number">01</div>
<h3>Sensing</h3>
<p>Collect LiDAR scans, camera frames, and IMU motion measurements from the stock vehicle platform.</p>
:::

::: {.pipeline-step}
<div class="step-number">02</div>
<h3>Track Understanding</h3>
<p>Estimate free space, wall boundaries, lane-like structure, or a target corridor that the vehicle can safely follow.</p>
:::

::: {.pipeline-step}
<div class="step-number">03</div>
<h3>Planning</h3>
<p>Generate a steering objective such as the largest safe gap, a lookahead waypoint, a local centerline, or a curvature-aware driving target.</p>
:::

::: {.pipeline-step}
<div class="step-number">04</div>
<h3>Control</h3>
<p>Convert the target into steering and speed commands while balancing tracking accuracy, smoothness, and stability through turns.</p>
:::
:::

::: {.content-text}
A practical final system may be <strong>reactive</strong> for simplicity and reliability, or it may combine reactive logic with a more structured planner for higher speed. Either way, the most important measure is how the full stack behaves on the car, not how elegant a single module looks in isolation.
:::

:::

::: {.section-band .section-band-dark}
::: {#demo .section-container}

## Demo Video {.section-title .section-title-light}

::: {.video-container .video-frame}
{{< video https://www.youtube.com/embed/cSQTZoZPJzs >}}
:::

::: {.content-text .content-text-light}
This section is where visitors can immediately see the car’s behavior on track: cornering smoothness, steering stability, throttle confidence, and overall speed. For a final submission, replace or supplement the current video with a race clip, onboard POV, and a short explanation of what the viewer should notice.
:::

:::
:::

::: {#results .section-container}

## Results & Evaluation {.section-title}

::: {.metrics-grid}
::: {.metric-card}
<div class="metric-label">Best Lap Time</div>
<div class="metric-value">TBD</div>
<div class="metric-note">Insert your best official or practice run time here.</div>
:::

::: {.metric-card}
<div class="metric-label">Completion Rate</div>
<div class="metric-value">TBD</div>
<div class="metric-note">Track how often the car finishes a run without failure.</div>
:::

::: {.metric-card}
<div class="metric-label">Controller Smoothness</div>
<div class="metric-value">TBD</div>
<div class="metric-note">Use steering variance, jerk, or qualitative stability notes.</div>
:::

::: {.metric-card}
<div class="metric-label">Top Reliable Speed</div>
<div class="metric-value">TBD</div>
<div class="metric-note">Record the fastest stable speed profile you can repeat.</div>
:::
:::

::: {.content-text}
A strong evaluation should combine <strong>speed</strong> and <strong>robustness</strong>. The best system is not necessarily the one with the single most aggressive run, but the one that repeatedly produces fast, clean laps. Consider presenting results with lap times, completion rate, controller smoothness, and failure cases such as wall approaches, steering oscillation, or late braking near tight turns.
:::

::: {.comparison-table-wrap}
| Category | Baseline Controller | Final Controller |
|---|---:|---:|
| Mean lap time | TBD | TBD |
| Best lap time | TBD | TBD |
| Successful runs / total | TBD | TBD |
| Max stable speed | TBD | TBD |
| Steering smoothness | TBD | TBD |
:::

:::

::: {.section-band}
::: {#engineering .section-container}

## Engineering Highlights {.section-title}

::: {.feature-grid}
::: {.feature-card}
### Robustness First
Before pushing for maximum speed, we prioritize reliable sensing and stable control. This reduces crashes and makes later performance tuning more meaningful.
:::

::: {.feature-card}
### Latency-Aware Design
Every extra processing step has a control cost. We choose algorithms that are not only accurate, but also fast enough for repeated real-time updates.
:::

::: {.feature-card}
### Test-Refine Loop
On-track iteration matters. Parameter tuning, corner-case debugging, and failure analysis usually determine race performance more than theory alone.
:::
:::

:::
:::

::: {#future .section-container}

## Future Improvements {.section-title}

::: {.content-text}
Natural next steps include adding stronger sensor fusion, using adaptive speed control based on curvature or confidence, improving recovery behavior when the car drifts away from the intended path, and visualizing the full autonomy pipeline in real time. If the current system is LiDAR-heavy, a camera-guided line or cone interpretation module could provide extra context. If the current system is largely reactive, a learned or map-informed planner could improve speed on repeated runs.
:::

:::

::: {.section-container}

## Project Resources {.section-title}

::: {.resource-grid}
::: {.resource-card}
**Codebase**  
Implementation and website repository:  
[project-site-invincible_proj](https://github.com/safeautonomy-illinois-students/project-site-invincible_proj/)
:::

::: {.resource-card}
**Demo Media**  
Current embedded video:  
[YouTube Demo](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cSQTZoZPJzs)
:::

::: {.resource-card}
**What to Add Next**  
Race images, trajectory plots, lap-time tables, ROS graph snapshots, and annotated failure cases.
:::
:::

:::

::: {.section-container}

## BibTeX {.section-title}

```bibtex
@misc{zhang2026invincibleproj,
  title        = {invincible\_proj: Autonomous F1TENTH Time Trial Racing},
  author       = {Zhenyu Zhang and Yuanzhe Wang and Murphy Huang},
  year         = {2026},
  note         = {ECE 484 final project website},
  howpublished = {GitHub Pages project site}
}
